codes.summary: CoC advertises PLE hiring, recruits PLE into committees and planning groups, and a PLE group decides which projects receive Unsheltered NOFO funding; governance document is being rewritten to formalize PLE roles.
evidence:
When hiring for CoC staff positions, we specifically advertise seeking people
            with lived experience of homelessness (PLE’s).

When building or rebuilding CoC Committees, the CoC recruits from
            participants or former participants

A group of PLE’s working on the overall Unsheltered Planning process is
            writing the Community Plan itself, designed the RFP for Unsheltered NOFO
            projects, and will determine what projects are submitted for funding.

codes.summary: Member agencies employ PLE (CPS peer specialists, HABD staff, etc.), with internal training on technology/leadership and a paid Certified Peer Specialist certification pathway; no CoC-wide compensation policy described.
evidence:
One Roof hires PLE’s and works with them to identify strengths and
            weaknesses and provides, to the limits of our budget, what is needed to fill the
            gaps including training on technology, leadership skills

Peer staff complete paid training and earn a certificate Certified Peer
            Specialist (CPS).

Several member agencies look first to program graduates when hiring staff
            or specify lived experience as a hiring preference.

codes.summary: CoC runs quarterly focus groups and anonymous surveys, ties provider scoring to feedback-gathering, and has translated recurring client concerns (ID access, felon-friendly housing) into concrete policy and landlord-outreach changes.
evidence:
We have put a schedule in place for this feedback gathering to continue at least
            quarterly.

Obtaining ID has been expressed as a major challenge in this CoC, and
            lack of ID slows or stops clients from signing leases. We have worked with the
            State Department of Public Safety (ID’s) to change policies

In response to this need One Roof put together an
            affordable housing list that is widely distributed and has a special section for
            felon-friendly housing.

codes.summary: CoC has 1 PLE on the Board and 9 PWLE voting members including on CE and Funding committees; requires providers to maintain a consumer-participation policy; a proposed participation fund to pay PLE was pending Board approval at time of writing.
evidence:
Currently the CoC has 1 self-identified
               person with lived experience on the CoC Board and 9 self- identified PWLE who
               are voting members.

the CoC Lead has drafted a consumer participation fund proposal for
               approval by the CoC Board at their October 2022 meeting.

the CoC
               required providers to have a consumer participation policy

codes.summary: Three Coordinated-Entry staff are PWLE; member agencies (Ministry of Caring, YWCA, WENH) route PLE into CNA/CDL/ECE certification and paid training pipelines via multi-org partnerships.
evidence: Three CE staff are PWLE.

Ministry of Caring
           (MOC), a CoC funded provider, operates job placement center connects
           homeless program clients w/ free certification training as Certified Nursing
           Assistants, Commercial Driving Licensure, & Early Childhood Education.

Participants enrolled w/ WENH have access to paid
           agricultural employment training

codes.summary: CoC formally collects feedback via PWLE voting members and a statewide survey informing HOME-ARP priorities; PLE-identified gap in housing navigation led New Castle County to fund a full-time housing locator, and ERA-application difficulty led to a new community navigation program.
evidence:
The DE CoC formally gathers feedback from PWLE who are a part of the
           voting membership, CoC Board, and CoC Cmtes.

Housing navigation services were identified by PWLE and people working
           directly with clients, as a major gap/need in our community... New Castle
           County funded and hired a full-time housing locator

the ERA administrator developed a community
           navigation program that funds staff in communities throughout the CoC to work
           directly with people in need of ERA assistance

codes.summary: Atlanta CoC has two PLE workgroups (CAC for adults, YAB for youth), both with voting representation on the Governing Council; YAB approved the YHDP Coordinated Community Plan and selected its subgrantees.
evidence:
The Atlanta Continuum of Care has two workgroups, the Client Advisory
               Council (CAC) and Youth Action Board (YAB), that consist of persons with lived
               experience.

Both groups
               have representation on the CoC Governing Council and have an active vote on
               all CoC matters as part of their inclusion.

the YAB leading the process for the development of the
               Coordinated Community Plan as part of YHDP funding. The group not only
               approved the final plan, but also worked with the CoC to review and select
               subgrantees

codes.summary: Several CoC partner agencies employ PLE in named substantive roles (Community Advocate, Program Coordinator, Teachers, Outreach Worker); a Peer Support Specialist scholarship program is being pursued but not yet implemented.
evidence:
Several of our CoC partners employ individuals with lived experience including,
            but not limited to, the Atlanta Volunteer Lawyers Foundation (Community
            Advocate); Our House (Program Coordinator and Teachers); Intown
            Collaborative Ministries (Outreach Worker).

The CoC is currently pursuing
            funding opportunities to implement a scholarship program for persons with lived
            experience to become Peer Support Specialists

codes.summary: CAC and YAB workgroups meet at least monthly; CAC is developing a 'secret shopper' program and hotline to collect and respond to complaints, and members review funding applications — concrete feedback-driven policy changes are prospective rather than executed.
evidence:
Both workgroups meet a minimum
            of once per month to provide feedback to the CoC.

The CAC is developing a “secret
            shopper” effort to survey emergency shelters and score them

members of the CAC and YAB serve as
            application reviewers for funding opportunities made available by City, State
            and Federal sources.

codes.summary: MIBOSCOC has six PWLE 'Subject Matter Experts,' each with a Governance Council seat and committee access, recruited via a published flier explicitly stating compensation rates; one serves as Executive Officer and two on the Funding Committee determining CoC program awards.
evidence:
creating
               a flier targeted towards PWLE explaining what MIBOSCOC is, how they can
               participate, and the rates at which they will be compensated for their time and
               effort.

Each PWLE who joins has a seat on the Governance Council and is
               invited to join any committees they have interest in.

One serves as an Executive Officer, another has been
               contracted to lead youth listening sessions, and two have served on the
               Funding Committee determining CoC Program funding decisions.

codes.summary: CoC pays PLE 'SMEs' at a professional-services rate with stipends for conferences, runs an LMS with paid learning time, and supports shadow-to-role pathways (e.g., grant writing) alongside substantive posts (Executive Officer, Funding Committee).
evidence:
supports those with lived
               expertise of homelessness in growing professionally and pays for their time at a
               professional services rate.

MIBOSCOC has an
               extensive Learning Management System (LMS) that all members are able to
               access and can be compensated for the time they spend learning.

One SME has
               been in discussions to shadow and learn about the grant writing process and
               take on tasks related to the next YHDP application process.

codes.summary: CoC runs surveys and interviews alongside provider-mandated feedback collection; initial youth survey results surfaced a gap in provider SOGIE training, which the CoC then sought additional training to address.
evidence:
service providers are required to solicit feedback from
            participants.

The MIBOSCOC has engaged in surveys and
            interviews with people who have engaged with the homeless system

From our first youth
            survey attempts we learned that providers needed additional training in sexual
            orientation, gender identity and expression and were able to seek more training
            out.

codes.summary: CoC continuously solicits PLE for Board/committees/PLE Workgroup via its website and targeted provider outreach; a named compensation policy is highlighted at every meeting, with targeted recruitment to BIPOC-serving providers.
evidence:
The CoC seeks Board/Cte/Workgroup membership from people with lived
           expertise of homelessness on an ongoing basis

This membership solicitation is posted on the CoC’s website
           continuously, and CoC staff highlight the ongoing membership solicitation and
           the CoC’s compensation policy in all meetings and trainings

CoC staff did targeted outreach to providers serving clientele with longer histories of
           homelessness and to those serving a larger proportion of BIPOC clients

codes.summary: CoC Written Homeless Program Standards encourage providers to offer PD and hire PLE; CoC directly paid for YAB professional development and provides in-depth training to its PLE Workgroup; providers report formal hiring preferences for past-experience applicants.
evidence:
Through the CoC’s Written Homeless Program Standards, the CoC has strongly
               encouraged all providers to provide professional development and employment
               opportunities to PLE.

specifically supported and paid for
               professional development opportunities for YAB members

providers report that they have added factors related to past experiences
               of homelessness into their formal applicant review process when seeking to hire
               new staff

codes.summary: Written Standards require all CoC-funded projects to seat PLE on their policy-making body and run formal feedback processes; the CoC-convened PLE Workgroup meets weekly and co-developed a project review tool that now guides funding prioritization and scoring.
evidence:
CoC’s written Homeless Program Standards require all CoC funded
           projects to include PLE on their agency board or equivalent policy-making body.

The CoC also convened a new PLE Workgroup
           in 2022; this group meets weekly.

CoC staff also recently worked with the PLE Workgroup to
           develop a project review tool based on their priorities. This tool helped establish
           new project priorities that the CoC team shared when soliciting new project
           proposals

codes.summary: GRCoC stood up a Youth Action Board to institutionalize youth voice, with a YAB representative seated on both the CoC Board and Ranking & Review Committee to guide planning and funding decisions.
evidence:
prioritized the development of a Youth Action
               Board and included a YAB representative in the Ranking & Review Committee
               and CoC Board to guide community planning

institutionalize youth voice in
               community decision-making processes.

The CoC incorporates underrepresented groups as critical stakeholders in any
               planning process and creates decision-making infrastructure that reflects this
               priority.

codes.summary: CoC reserves a YAB seat on the Ranking Committee (treated as PD opportunity) and runs a strategic-planning steering committee where one-third of seats are PLE, giving exposure to a broader professional network — but no paid posts or training pipeline described.
evidence:
development of a Youth Action Board (YAB) includes a policy
            that reserves one seat on the CoC Ranking Committee for one member of the
            YAB.

steering
            committee, which is comprised of 1/3 of people with lived experience of
            homelessness.

gives them exposure to a broad professional network of CoC
            board members working in the fields of local government, philanthropy, and
            affordable housing.

codes.summary: Five PLE focus groups in last 12 months surfaced rental assistance and housing navigation as top unmet needs; CoC responded by pursuing Stability Vouchers and a centralized navigation project via the Supplemental CoC NOFO.
evidence:
the GRCoC has had five focus groups comprised
            of people currently experiencing or with lived experience of homelessness.

The most common input on unmet service needs were
            rental assistance and more one-on-one housing navigation. In response, the
            CoC is partnering with the largest area Public Housing Authority to apply for
            Stability Vouchers.
CoC is submitting a Supplemental CoC NOFO application that includes a project for centralized housing navigation for unsheltered households.

codes.summary: CoC Governance Charter requires PLE on the Board; Ad Hoc Nomination Committee includes a PLE member; a YAB made up exclusively of PLE (chaired by a Board Director) is embedded in governance; CoC leadership team includes a PLE whose input shapes policy.
evidence:
The CoC Governance Charter also includes language ensuring the CoC Board
               includes members with direct lived experience.

an Ad Hoc
               Nomination Committee, which includes at least one individual with lived
               experience, works through local providers to identify and recruit members for
               that Board.

the CoC’s leadership team included a person
               with lived experience whose input into policy development, strategy, and
               implementation was routinely solicited and incorporated into decision making

codes.summary: Shelters, TH and PSH providers routinely hire PLE and offer pathways to leadership and management; external workforce agencies (Worknet, CalWORKS) supply vocational services, but no CoC-specific training pipeline or compensation policy described.
evidence:
Many CoC members routinely hire individuals with a lived experience of
            homelessness; this is especially true of emergency shelters, transitional
            housing providers, and permanent supportive housing providers.

the organizations provide pathways to
            develop professionally, build work experience, and move into positions of
            leadership and management.

codes.summary: Shelter and Street Outreach committees have Board-adopted feedback mandates with ~50% PLE membership; Strategic Planning ran four PLE-exclusive listening sessions; gathered input has informed coordinated entry and HMIS policy updates and funding allocations.
evidence:
Two CoC Committees, Shelter and Street Outreach, have
            Board-adopted mandates centered around improving engagement of those with
            lived experience, and about half of those committees’ members have lived
            experience.

The Strategic Planning Committee engaged in four listening sessions
            in the first quarter of 2022 which focused exclusively on those with lived
            experience.

Determinations gathered from the abovementioned strategies have been
            used to varying degrees to inform reviews and updates of policies and
            procedures related to coordinated entry and HMIS

codes.summary: CoC recruited a designated Board seat for a person with homeless experience, seated a chartered YAB, and embedded a PLE in the leadership team whose input is routinely incorporated into decision making; recruitment uses social media and partner orgs.
evidence:
Recruited members for the CoC’s Youth Action Board (YAB) - a
               chartered CoC Committee that advises the CoC on youth-specific policy and
               initiatives - via engagement with organizations that work with youth with lived
               experience and recruitment calls on social media

Recruited
               for a designated Board seat for a person with homeless experience

the CoC’s leadership team included a person
               with lived experience whose input into policy development, strategy, and
               implementation was routinely solicited and incorporated into decision making

codes.summary: PLE are employed in direct service and management positions at the CoC's largest drop-in shelter and largest youth provider; professional development is delivered via direct supervision, in-service training, and continuing education; LHC encourages PLE to apply for open positions via job postings.
evidence:
employing
              people with lived experience in direct service and management positions at the
              CoC's largest drop-in shelter and largest youth service provider

professional
              development is provided via direct supervision, in-service training, and
              continuing education.

Louisiana Housing Corporation, the CoC’s collaborative applicant, provides
              capacity building, leadership, and soft skill development to people with lived
              experience

codes.summary: Street outreach staff report feedback at biweekly network meetings alongside monthly participant surveys and exit interviews; PLE feedback has driven new landlord engagement, affordable-housing advocacy, and faster outreach-to-housing handoffs; case-management practices explicitly reduce engagement barriers.
evidence:
street outreach staff who share this feedback at biweekly street
            outreach network meetings.

implementing case management
            practices that are flexible, assertive, and client centered, reducing barriers to
            engagement by coordinating with street outreach teams to connect with hard to
            reach unsheltered participants

Feedback from people in unsheltered
            situations informed new processes to increase coordination between street
            outreach and housing projects to decrease time between referral and housing
            move in

codes.summary: ~9% of the CoC Board (approx. 11 members) have experienced homelessness; a long-time consumer is an elected HSPC Officer; Timao Center runs a paid unsheltered summer work group; opportunities disseminated via meetings, social media, and fliers.
evidence:
The CoC Board has about 11 participants (9%) on the Homeless
               Service Providers Coalition and/or the Mayor’s Task Force to End
               Homelessness who have experienced homelessness

Timao Center Drop-in, a day shelter, has a temporary summer work group
               comprised of unsheltered homeless persons that are paid to provide clean up
               around the city.
A long-time consumer has been elected as an Officer on the HSPC.

codes.summary: CoC and ESG-funded programs are required to involve participants in employment or volunteer services; partners span from paid summer work groups through MassHire, YouthBuild, HealthFirst, and substance-use-peer-coach pipelines including outreach workers and recovery coaches.
evidence:
Timao Center Drop-in, a day shelter, in conjunction with the Mayor’s office and
           the Community Development Agency, has a temporary summer work group
           comprised of unsheltered homeless persons that are paid

Many local organizations that address substance abuse disorders provide
           training and employment for positions at their organizations such as outreach
           workers, recovery coaches, shelter staff, program staff, one-on-one peer
           coaches

It is a requirement that CoC and ESG funded programs involve their participants
           through employment or volunteer services

codes.summary: Feedback collected via advisory boards, tenant/participant meetings, annual Project Homeless Connect exit surveys, biannual ESG public hearings, and CES client surveys; feedback is said to inform policy but no specific enacted change is cited.
evidence:
Local CoC programs convene advisory boards that include people with lived
           experience.

Exit surveys for consumers and outcome forms for providers at the annual
           Project Homeless Connect

Biannual Public Hearings for ESG funding held by FRCDA to communicate to
           the public and solicit public opinion regarding community needs including
           homelessness

codes.summary: CoC has a current PLE board member in a decision-making role, pays $50/hr to contracted PLE staff (via gift card or check), and sources PLE through partner agencies; plans to use listening-session input to revise shelter-stay policies and CES design.
evidence:
COC has a current board member that
               has lived experience so they are in a leadership/decision making role within the
               COC.

COC pays $50.00 per hour for any contracted
               staff that have lived experience and offers a variety of ways to receive
               compensation, i.e visa gift card or check.

With the feedback and
               responses from the listening session the CoC will be looking into specific
               policies that are based on shelter stay/length of time in shelter

codes.summary: CoC pays $50/hr stipends to a Lived Experience Cohort (quarterly) and a partner provider pays $75/month for board and task-force participation; 'lived experience with homelessness' is a preferred hiring qualification and employment coaching is available via a Center for Working Families program.
evidence:
The COC is
           able to offer stipends for this work or their expertise at a rate of $50 per hour
           and usually has that participation at least quarterly.

One of the PSH/RRH/TH providers includes “lived
           experience with homelessness” as a preferred qualification on all of their job
           postings.

They compensate them for their time at a rate of $75/month to prepare for monthly
           meetings and participate in meetings.

codes.summary: Follow-up surveys every 180 days plus third-party listening sessions targeting shelter/ESG participants; an Outcomes Committee reviews survey instruments; discussed actions are prospective (web app, policy revisions) rather than enacted changes.
evidence:
agencies also send out follow up assistance
           needed/check in surveys every 180 days.

COC also has set up a third party
           listening sessions so current/past participants can freely express their thoughts

CoC
           Outcomes Committee consistently goes through the follow up surveys ensuring
           that questions are still applicable

codes.summary: PLE seated on CoC Board, across YAB (responsible for planning youth services and reviewing performance), and other planning committees; every CoC provider required to seat PLE on their own Board; new lived-experience committee added; recruitment via social media and peer referrals.
evidence:
This includes membership on the CoC Board, the entirety of the Youth Action Board
               which is responsible for planning youth services and reviewing performance,
               and other planning committees

every CoC provider organization is
               required to have someone with lived experience on their Board as well.

The CoC uses program feedback sessions, social media, street outreach and peer

codes.summary: Workforce-board and SNAP E&T partnerships feed PLE into employment and job-training pipelines; partner social enterprises (Curbside Chronicle newspaper, flower shop, T-shirt shop, Sasquatch shaved ice, Better Way day-work program) hire homeless and formerly homeless individuals.
evidence:
The CoC partners with the Central Oklahoma Workforce Innovation Board and
           Public Strategies SNAP Employment and Training Program.

Curbside Chronicle is a street paper that is
           written and sold by homeless individuals.

Better Way program was launched last year. The program
           provides a day’s work to people who are homeless and/or panhandling and
           connects them with an employment specialist

codes.summary: Consumer-satisfaction surveys are scored in provider applications plus a newly seated lived-experience committee; feedback that longer-term rapid re-housing and intensive case management were needed prompted a 2-year Clutch Consulting contract to build those resources.
evidence:
The CoC also recently seated a lived experience committee to provide ongoing
           feedback into CoC, ESG and other services.

feedback
           indicated that longer term rapid re-housing assistance would be more useful in
           helping individuals and families stabilize

The CoC recently engaged Clutch consulting to assist with
           building more resources to provide longer term rapid re-housing assistance

codes.summary: PLE involved at every level of KVC including staff, AmeriCorps positions, Board of Directors, and member-agency boards; outreach is ad hoc via agency and program-level announcements rather than public advertisements.
evidence:
Individuals with lived experience are involved with every level of the KVC
               including: KVC Staff, Board of Directors, Americorps positions, member agency
               staff, and board members.

Outreach efforts include announcements at
               agencies, and program level invitations.

The clients are very good at providing feedback along
               with their input as to how to make things better and changes that need to be
               made to meet their needs.

codes.summary: Margaret Taylor Job Readiness Center runs HiSET prep, computer and office training, career development, life-skills coaching, and transportation; AmeriCorps PLE positions at KVC receive training including financial literacy.
evidence:
Annually the Margaret Taylor Job Readiness Center provides the following
           education and employment skills to nearly 400 homeless and/or at-risk of
           becoming homeless, including Veterans: Pre-HiSET classes, computers and
           office technology, Adult Basic education/Adult Literacy

Transportation is also available
           for program participants.

Americorps serving through the KVC go through a variety of trainings including
           financial literacy

codes.summary: A 15-member PLE committee reviewed KVC goals and each program's NOFO application; exit surveys feed team discussions; specific changes (adding tutors, expanding job search to higher-wage jobs) were driven by PLE input.
evidence:
The KVC has a Person’s with Lived Experience committee (PLE) consisting
           15 individuals who are currently or have formerly received services from one or
           more COC / ESG funded programs.

This committee reviewed the goals of the
           KVC in regards to ending homelessness, and reviewed each of the programs
           seeking funding through the NOFO process.

Some examples have been bringing in tutors to assist
           with learning, broadening the job search areas to include higher waged jobs

codes.summary: Lee County CoC has grown PLE representation on the Governing Board and added a standing lived-experience committee with subcommittee participation; the local CoC Ranking process formally awards points to agencies that involve PLE in board membership, employment, and program development.
evidence:
representation of individuals with lived experiences has increased on
            the Governing Board.

The CoC has also formed a standing lived experience
            committee and ensured participation in subcommittees.

the local CoC Ranking process awards points to agencies that
            effectively involve individuals with lived experiences in board membership,
            employment, and program development.

codes.summary: CoC-funded agencies employ PLE in outreach, peer navigation, and support roles; the Ranking process awards additional points for agencies advancing PLE; approved projects allocate funding for PD including skill training, internships, and continuing education.
evidence:
Several CoC-funded agencies employ staff with lived experience in
               roles essential to programs including positions in outreach, peer
               navigation and support services.

the CoC Ranking process awards
               additional points to agencies demonstrating a commitment to employing and
               advancing individuals with lived experience.

This initiative is supported by
               funding from approved projects that allocate resources specifically for
               professional development activities. These include skill-based training,
               internships and continuing education

codes.summary: Feedback flows through monthly CoC General Membership meetings, individual satisfaction surveys, daily outreach, and a 24/7 Coordinated Entry phone line; PLE input drove expansion of the outreach team, trauma-informed training for providers, and CE wait-time reductions.
evidence:
Feedback is gathered frequently through various channels: monthly during
            CoC General Membership meetings, through regular individual satisfaction
            surveys, daily direct outreach efforts, and continuously via the 24/7 Coordinated
            Entry phone line.

expanding the outreach team with additional staff to enhance
            engagement and support. Training for service providers has been improved,
            focusing on trauma-informed care and cultural competency

Improvements to the
            Coordinated Entry process have been made to reduce wait times and simplify
            access to services.

codes.summary: Annual Housing & Homelessness Summit features PLE keynote speakers shaping the Strategic Action Framework; three PLE participants have agreed to join the Guam Homeless Coalition's decision-making process, and several member orgs employ PLE peer-support specialists.
evidence:
During the summit persons with lived experience give critical input into crafting the goals
               and actions steps of the Strategic Action Framework, which guides Guam’s
               coordinated response to homelessness.

we have
               three participants that have and will participate in the Guam Homeless Coalition
               meetings and have agreed to be part of the decision making process of the
               Coalition.

Several member organizations partner with or employ persons with lived
               experience who are peer support specialists.

codes.summary: Training pipelines for homeless individuals run through Guam DOL (DVOP for veterans, apprenticeship programs, reentry services), WestCare Pacific Islands (HVRP-funded skills training, job readiness, mentorship), and Manelu (Employment Empowerment Initiative with language translation); narrative focuses on training delivered to homeless clients rather than PLE in CoC staff positions.
evidence:
DOL supports registered apprenticeship
           programs across various industries in partnership with government, private, and
           non-profit organizations that homeless individuals can participate in these
           programs.

WestCare Pacific Islands
           established a full-scale employment program with HVRP funding through the
           Department of Labor. This program provides professional development and
           employment opportunities to individuals with lived experience of homelessness

Manelu also runs the Employment Empowerment Initiative that provides training
           and support to individuals who have lost jobs

codes.summary: Feedback collected via the Outreach Committee and Guahan Youth Initiative Board (each seating 2 PLE), quarterly program reports, monthly general membership meetings, and periodic focus groups; PLE input led to an open grievance policy for all project-level agencies.
evidence:
Guam’s CoC gathers feedback from the member organizations specifically
           through the Outreach Committee and Guahan Youth Initiative Board, composed
           of individuals with lived experience of homelessness.

Feedback from individuals receiving CoC or ESG assistance is collected
           quarterly through program reports as part of their program evaluations

Based on feedback, our CoC has implemented several measures, including
           establishing an open grievance policy for all project-level agencies

codes.summary: CoC recruited a designated Board seat for a PLE, nominated a PLE member for the statewide Interagency Council on Homelessness, runs a chartered YAB, and routinely solicits PLE Board-member input on policy development; recruitment happens via social media, CoC meetings, email, and web postings.
evidence:
recruited for a

CoC members with lived
               experience serve on CoC Committees and participate in policy development

The CoC has nominated a CoC member with lived expertise to

codes.summary: PLE are employed in direct service and management positions at the CoC's largest drop-in shelter and largest youth provider; YWCA and Christian Outreach Center hire PLE in entry-through-director-level positions; LHC provides capacity-building and leadership development.
evidence:
employing people with lived experience in direct service and management
            positions at the CoC’s largest drop-in shelter and largest youth provider.

            Outreach Center employ people with lived experience in entry and director level
            positions

Louisiana Housing Corporation, the CoC’s collaborative applicant provides

codes.summary: Dense feedback architecture: biweekly street-outreach network meetings, monthly CoC meetings, quarterly Board meetings, bimonthly regional subcommittee, annual participant interviews, plus YAB and CE Local Competition Scoring inputs; PLE input drove flexible/strengths-based case management and faster outreach-to-housing handoff.
evidence:
Feedback from people with lived experience occurs at biweekly street

implementing case management

improving coordination between street outreach and housing projects to reduce time

codes.summary: CoC (in partnership with the City of Cambridge) involves PLE on committees and working groups and offers incentive payments for participation; recruitment uses flyers, trusted providers, and targeted outreach; 1D-10a chart shows 3 PLE routinely included in decisionmaking processes.
evidence:
Outreach has

The CoC in
               partnership with the City of Cambridge offers incentive payments for individuals

               groups
Routinely included in the decisionmaking processes related to addressing homelessness.

codes.summary: Mass Rehab Commission partners to recruit PLE interns; Cambridge Employment Program/Cambridge Works provide transitional-jobs pathways; subrecipient agencies employ PLE staff at leadership and senior-management levels, with education requirements relaxed to credit lived experience.
evidence:
CoC
           agencies partner with local agencies such as the Mass Rehabilitation

The CoC Collaborative Applicant and CoC member agencies have worked to

codes.summary: Quarterly formal feedback cycles through Board, Committees, and Working Groups with paid focus groups; subrecipients run agency-level advisory boards and collect participant feedback via surveys; PLE input led to expanded shower services, more housing navigators, and increased meal availability.

The CoC gathers feedback from people experiencing homelessness formally

increasing the availability of shower services in the community, increasing the

codes.summary: CoC runs a Lived Experience Committee co-chaired by a group member; recruitment flyer was translated into Spanish to broaden access; committee is developing its own agenda and initiatives but no explicit CoC board-level decisionmaking role described.
evidence:
We developed a flyer
               (with a Spanish translation provided by the co-chair) for member agencies to

The committee now has an HLC co-

Lived
               Experience Committee growing, developing its own agenda, and moving toward

codes.summary: Anyone with lived experience participating in CoC efforts is paid via a tiered hourly compensation scale keyed to experience and duty assignments; new PLEE Advisory Board members are paired with experienced mentors for structured onboarding.
evidence:
Anyone with lived experience and expertise, including YAB members who
            participate in CoC efforts are compensated for their time via an hourly rate
            determined by a tiered compensation scale.

This scale is based upon
            experience and duty assignments and inherently provides an opportunity to
            increase pay by becoming more proficient at different tasks and committing
            more time to the work of the CoC.

New PLEE Advisory Board members are
            paired with a more experienced member who guides them through the Advisory
            Board work

codes.summary: PLE on DV, CE, and Advisory Board provide monthly feedback; YYAs drove successful YHSI and YHDP funding applications and participated in the Advisory Board Working Group that redesigned the CoC Governance Charter; ESG requires subrecipients to follow standards for involving PLE in program design.
evidence:
CoC Committee meetings where PLEE regularly provide feedback occur on
            a monthly basis.

Youth and Young Adults (YYAs) with lived experience were crucial
            earlier this year in developing an application for YHSI funding that was granted
            to MA as well as in the application process for the latest round of YHDP
            funding.

PLEE were important members of the Advisory Board Working
            Group to redesign the CoC Governance Charter this year.

codes.summary: CoC promoted open Board seats to PLE via direct outreach and publicly posted membership info, recruiting a PLEEH to the Board; a PWLE Advisory Committee is still being developed in collaboration with the Regional Racial Justice Advisory Committee.
evidence:
promotion of open board positions to those with lived experience via direct
               client outreach, and ensuring transparent and publicly posted Membership and
               Committee information for full public participation.

CoC Board members conduct
               targeted outreach to PLEEH within their own programs/agencies to fill board
               vacancies which resulted in a PLEEH recruitment to the board.

continuing the process for
               developing a Persons with Lived Experience (PWLE) Advisory Committee

codes.summary: St. Paul's Center employs 11 PLE across executive leadership and senior line-staff roles; Troy Savings Bank ambassadors program links PLE to hospitality employment; Regional Racial Justice Advisory Committee runs CoC Board 101 and Facilitation/Chairperson 101 training for PWLE.
evidence:
St. Paul’s Center directly hires
            clients/prior clients within its agency. To date the agency has eleven staff with
            lived experience in various positions including executive leadership and senior
            line-staff positions.

an ambassador’s program with
            Troy Savings Bank which links PWLE to employment opportunities for
            hospitality services to theater patrons & visitors to the downtown area.

The Regional Racial Justice Advisory Committee, which NY-512 participates in, has
            developed professional training for PWLE: CoC Board 101 sessions and
            Facilitation/Chairperson 101 series

codes.summary: Newly formed PWLE Committee will gather feedback quarterly alongside Strategic Planning Committee surveys and annual program-participant feedback; PLE-identified gaps in affordable housing and eviction prevention led the CoC to advocate for City of Troy and NYS Home-ARP priorities and to build landlord relationships via the Systems Committee.
evidence:
The CoC will gather feedback from the People with Lived
            Experience Committee quarterly.

Specific challenges
            identified by PWLE through surveys and focus groups were the lack of
            affordable housing and eviction prevention funding available. The CoC
            advocated for the City of Troy & NYS Home-ARP to prioritize emergency rental
            assistance

In response to PWLE feedback that affordable housing units are challenging to
            find even w/ rental assistance in hand, the CoC Systems Committee is working
            to cultivate relationships with landlords
